sections.title: Plants and Animals Live Together
sections.content_summary: This introductory section establishes the core idea that wherever plants grow, animals live on, around, and underneath them. A visual spread of twelve animals is presented — frog, tailorbird, elephants, squirrels, ants, caterpillar, butterfly, woodpecker, moth, barbet, stink bug, and earthworms — each shown in relation to specific plant parts. Students are invited to recognise these animals from their own surroundings and identify which live underground. A 'Do you know?' panel extends this: camels eat leaves, owls nest in tree hollows, squirrels and crows use twigs, monkeys eat fruits, sunbirds and butterflies drink nectar, tailorbirds stitch leaves for nests, bats and leopards rest on branches, and caterpillars eat leaves — illustrating how animals use plants for food, shelter, and rearing young.

tasks.task_text: Which of the animals (birds and insects) shown on page 63, have you seen before? Describe where and how you saw them. Use your fingers, hands and arms to show how small or big these animals are!
Why do these animals choose to live near plants?

sections.title: Life in the Soil
sections.content_summary: This section introduces soil as a living system — the topmost layer of the Earth's surface made from broken rock fragments, old leaves, roots, stems, and living and dead animals. Students explore soil near a plant through direct sensory observation: its colour, texture (rough, smooth, hard, grainy), moisture, smell, and the creatures within it. The section explains that soil near plants hosts insects such as ants, termites, beetles, and grasshoppers, and after rain, earthworms and millipedes emerge. Students compare soil from under a plant and soil away from plants, and investigate seasonal changes in animal and plant presence, particularly in the monsoon. Activity 3 builds auditory awareness: students listen to and record bird sounds at different times of day, practising sound localisation by cupping their ears.

tasks.task_text: Pick up a little soil near your plant friend with your hands. How does it look and feel? Is it dry, damp, rough, smooth, hard or grainy? Did you find any leaves or insects in the soil? Now smell this soil and remember it.
In the monsoons, you may find many more plants and animals around. Where did these new plants and animals come from? Why could you not see them earlier?
Stand near your plant friend and look around. How many different animals can you spot? Describe them in words and fill in the table. You may use the given pictures if needed.

Sounds of Birds. Close your eyes and try to listen to the sounds of birds. Do you hear any bird sounds? Can you recognise which birds are making these sounds? Cup your ears with your hands and point your face towards the direction of the bird sound. Can you hear the sound more clearly? Recall the bird sounds you have listened to. Try to produce the sounds that different birds make. Now try to write down in the table the sound of any birds you have heard. If you do not hear any sounds of birds, what do you think is the reason? Do you hear more bird sounds in the early morning, in the afternoon, or in the evening?

sections.title: Let us reflect
sections.content_summary: The reflection section consolidates learning from the chapter's activities through discussion, writing, drawing, and a sequencing task. Students discuss the soil specimens they collected, recall when new leaves appeared on their plant friend, and identify animals, birds, and insects they observed. They fill in a structured table describing two animals observed near plants — their shape, size, colour, and possible reasons for being near the plant. Students draw their plant friend and use coloured dots to mark where they spotted animals. A sequencing task using the story of Suma and a caterpillar guides students to put the stages of a caterpillar's life cycle in order, with the guidance of an elder person — embedding intergenerational and observational knowledge.

tasks.task_text: What was the colour and texture of the two soil specimens that you collected during the previous activity? In which month did you pick them up? Did they smell different and can you describe the smells?
During which month did fresh new leaves appear on the plant that you were observing?
Which animals, birds and insects did you notice around plants?
Recall and write about how animals, birds and insects rely on plants. Which one was your favourite example?
What is soil made up of?

Fill in the table: Names of two animals that you observed near or on plants. Describe the shape, size and colours of these animals. Guess why these animals were staying near these plants. What other interesting things did you notice about these animals?
Remember you wrote about your plant friend. Now try and draw your plant friend. Use different coloured small dots in your drawing to show the places where you spotted animals, birds or insects.

Put in order. Consult an elder person and organise the following changes in order: Now it was a butterfly. The caterpillar formed a cocoon. It flew away gently. One day it stopped eating. It came out of the cocoon.

dual_strand.natural: Animals, insects, and birds living in and around plants; soil as a living ecosystem composed of rock, plant matter, and living organisms
dual_strand.human_cultural: Human observation and curiosity as pathways to understanding nature; intergenerational knowledge (consulting elders); attentiveness to bird sounds and seasonal rhythms
